name: array-api-compat

metadata.name: array-api-compat
metadata.summary: A wrapper around NumPy and other array libraries to make them compatible with the Array API standard

documentation:
# Array API compatibility library

This is a small wrapper around common array libraries that is compatible with
the [Array API standard](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/). Currently,
NumPy, CuPy, PyTorch, Dask, JAX, ndonnx and `sparse` are supported. If you want
support for other array libraries, or if you encounter any issues, please [open
an issue](https://github.com/data-apis/array-api-compat/issues).

See the documentation for more details https://data-apis.org/array-api-compat/

modules.name: array_api_compat._internal
documentation: Internal helpers

functions.name: array_api_compat._internal.get_xp
array_api_compat.dask.array.linalg.qr
documentation: TODO: use the QR wrapper once dask
supports the mode keyword on QR
https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/10388
qr = get_xp(da)(_linalg.qr)

functions.name: array_api_compat.dask.array.linalg.svd
documentation: Wrap the svd functions to not pass full_matrices to dask
when full_matrices=False (as that is the default behavior for dask),
and dask doesn't have the full_matrices keyword

functions.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._aliases._dask_arange
documentation: Common aliases
This arange func is modified from the common one to
not pass stop/step as keyword arguments, which will cause
an error with dask
TODO: delete the xp stuff, it shouldn't be necessary

functions.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._aliases.asarray
documentation:
Array API compatibility wrapper for asarray().

See the corresponding documentation in the array library and/or the array API
specification for more details.
asarray also adds the copy keyword, which is not present in numpy 1.0.

functions.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._aliases.clip
documentation: dask.array.clip does not work unless all three arguments are provided.
Furthermore, the masking workaround in common._aliases.clip cannot work with
dask (meaning uint64 promoting to float64 is going to just be unfixed for
now).

modules.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._info
documentation:
Array API Inspection namespace

This is the namespace for inspection functions as defined by the array API
standard. See
https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/inspection.html for
more details.

classes.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._info.__array_namespace_info__

methods.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._info.__array_namespace_info__.capabilities
documentation:
Return a dictionary of array API library capabilities.

The resulting dictionary has the following keys:

- **"boolean indexing"**: boolean indicating whether an array library
  supports boolean indexing. Always `False` for Dask.
- **"data-dependent shapes"**: boolean indicating whether an array
  library supports data-dependent output shapes. Always `False` for
  Dask.

See
<https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.info.capabilities.html>
for more details.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Returns

capabilities

: A dictionary of array API library capabilities.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.capabilities()
{'boolean indexing': True,
 'data-dependent shapes': True}
```

methods.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._info.__array_namespace_info__.default_device
documentation:
The default device used for new Dask arrays.

For Dask, this always returns `'cpu'`.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Returns

device

: The default device used for new Dask arrays.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.default_device()
'cpu'
```

methods.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._info.__array_namespace_info__.default_dtypes
documentation:
The default data types used for new Dask arrays.

For Dask, this always returns the following dictionary:

- **"real floating"**: `numpy.float64`
- **"complex floating"**: `numpy.complex128`
- **"integral"**: `numpy.intp`
- **"indexing"**: `numpy.intp`

# Parameters

device

: The device to get the default data types for.

# Returns

dtypes

: A dictionary describing the default data types used for new Dask
  arrays.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.default_dtypes()
{'real floating': dask.float64,
 'complex floating': dask.complex128,
 'integral': dask.int64,
 'indexing': dask.int64}
```

methods.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._info.__array_namespace_info__.dtypes
documentation:
The array API data types supported by Dask.

Note that this function only returns data types that are defined by
the array API.

# Parameters

device

: The device to get the data types for.

kind

: The kind of data types to return. If `None`, all data types are
  returned. If a string, only data types of that kind are returned.
  If a tuple, a dictionary containing the union of the given kinds
  is returned. The following kinds are supported:

  - `'bool'`: boolean data types (i.e., `bool`).
  - `'signed integer'`: signed integer data types (i.e., `int8`,
    `int16`, `int32`, `int64`).
  - `'unsigned integer'`: unsigned integer data types (i.e.,
    `uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `uint64`).
  - `'integral'`: integer data types. Shorthand for `('signed
    integer', 'unsigned integer')`.
  - `'real floating'`: real-valued floating-point data types
    (i.e., `float32`, `float64`).
  - `'complex floating'`: complex floating-point data types (i.e.,
    `complex64`, `complex128`).
  - `'numeric'`: numeric data types. Shorthand for `('integral',
    'real floating', 'complex floating')`.

# Returns

dtypes

: A dictionary mapping the names of data types to the corresponding
  Dask data types.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.dtypes(kind='signed integer')
{'int8': dask.int8,
 'int16': dask.int16,
 'int32': dask.int32,
 'int64': dask.int64}
```

methods.name: array_api_compat.dask.array._info.__array_namespace_info__.devices
documentation:
The devices supported by Dask.

For Dask, this always returns `['cpu', DASK_DEVICE]`.

# Returns

devices

: The devices supported by Dask.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.dtypes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.devices()
['cpu', DASK_DEVICE]
```

Get the array API inspection namespace for Dask.

The array API inspection namespace defines the following functions:

- capabilities()
- default_device()
- default_dtypes()
- dtypes()
- devices()

See
<https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/inspection.html>
for more details.

# Returns

info

: The array API inspection namespace for Dask.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.default_dtypes()
{'real floating': dask.float64,
 'complex floating': dask.complex128,
 'integral': dask.int64,
 'indexing': dask.int64}
```

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._linalg.cross
documentation: These are in the main NumPy namespace but not in numpy.linalg

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._linalg.eigh
documentation: These functions are the same as their NumPy counterparts except they return
a namedtuple.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._linalg.cholesky
documentation: These functions have additional keyword arguments
The upper keyword argument is new from NumPy

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._linalg.matrix_rank
documentation: The rtol keyword argument of matrix_rank() and pinv() is new from NumPy.
Note that it has a different semantic meaning from tol and rcond.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._linalg.matrix_norm
documentation: These functions are new in the array API spec

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._linalg.svdvals
documentation: svdvals is not in NumPy (but it is in SciPy). It is equivalent to
xp.linalg.svd(compute_uv=False).

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._linalg.diagonal
documentation: xp.diagonal and xp.trace operate on the first two axes whereas these
operates on the last two

modules.name: array_api_compat
documentation:
NumPy Array API compatibility library

This is a small wrapper around NumPy and CuPy that is compatible with the
Array API standard https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/. See also NEP 47
https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0047-array-api-standard.html.

Unlike array_api_strict, this is not a strict minimal implementation of the
Array API, but rather just an extension of the main NumPy namespace with
changes needed to be compliant with the Array API. See
https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/array_api.html for a full list of
changes. In particular, unlike array_api_strict, this package does not use a
separate Array object, but rather just uses numpy.ndarray directly.

Library authors using the Array API may wish to test against array_api_strict
to ensure they are not using functionality outside of the standard, but prefer
this implementation for the default when working with NumPy arrays.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._fft.fft
documentation: Note: NumPy fft functions improperly upcast float32 and complex64 to
complex128, which is why we require wrapping them all here.

modules.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers
documentation:
Various helper functions which are not part of the spec.

Functions which start with an underscore are for internal use only but helpers
that are in __all__ are intended as additional helper functions for use by end
users of the compat library.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers._is_jax_zero_gradient_array
documentation:
Return True if `x` is a zero-gradient array.

These arrays are a design quirk of Jax that may one day be removed.
See https://github.com/google/jax/issues/20620.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers.is_numpy_array
documentation:
Return True if `x` is a NumPy array.

This function does not import NumPy if it has not already been imported
and is therefore cheap to use.

This also returns True for `ndarray` subclasses and NumPy scalar objects.

See Also
--------

array_namespace
is_array_api_obj
is_cupy_array
is_torch_array
is_ndonnx_array
is_dask_array
is_jax_array
is_pydata_sparse_array

This also returns True for `cupy.ndarray` subclasses and CuPy scalar objects.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers.array_namespace
documentation:
Get the array API compatible namespace for the arrays `xs`.

# Parameters

xs: arrays

: one or more arrays.

api_version: str

: The newest version of the spec that you need support for (currently
  the compat library wrapped APIs support v2023.12).

use_compat: bool or None

: If None (the default), the native namespace will be returned if it is
  already array API compatible, otherwise a compat wrapper is used. If
  True, the compat library wrapped library will be returned. If False,
  the native library namespace is returned.

# Returns

out: namespace

: The array API compatible namespace corresponding to the arrays in `xs`.

# Raises

TypeError

: If `xs` contains arrays from different array libraries or contains a
  non-array.

Typical usage is to pass the arguments of a function to
`array_namespace()` at the top of a function to get the corresponding
array API namespace:

```python
def your_function(x, y):
    xp = array_api_compat.array_namespace(x, y)
    # Now use xp as the array library namespace
    return xp.mean(x, axis=0) + 2*xp.std(y, axis=0)
```

Wrapped array namespaces can also be imported directly. For example,
`array_namespace(np.array(...))` will return `array_api_compat.numpy`.
This function will also work for any array library not wrapped by
array-api-compat if it explicitly defines [\_\_array_namespace\_\_](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.array.__array_namespace__.html)
(the wrapped namespace is always preferred if it exists).

# See Also

is_array_api_obj
is_numpy_array
is_cupy_array
is_torch_array
is_dask_array
is_jax_array
is_pydata_sparse_array

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers.device
documentation:
Hardware device the array data resides on.

This is equivalent to `x.device` according to the [standard](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.array.device.html).
This helper is included because some array libraries either do not have
the `device` attribute or include it with an incompatible API.

# Parameters

x: array

: array instance from an array API compatible library.

# Returns

out: device

: a `device` object (see the [Device Support](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/design_topics/device_support.html)
  section of the array API specification).

# Notes

For NumPy the device is always `"cpu"`. For Dask, the device is always a
special `DASK_DEVICE` object.

# See Also

to_device : Move array data to a different device.
device() is not on numpy.ndarray or dask.array and to_device() is not on numpy.ndarray
or cupy.ndarray. They are not included in array objects of this library
because this library just reuses the respective ndarray classes without
wrapping or subclassing them. These helper functions can be used instead of
the wrapper functions for libraries that need to support both NumPy/CuPy and
other libraries that use devices.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers._cupy_to_device
documentation: Based on cupy.array_api.Array.to_device

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers.to_device
documentation:
Copy the array from the device on which it currently resides to the specified `device`.

This is equivalent to `x.to_device(device, stream=stream)` according to
the [standard](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.array.to_device.html).
This helper is included because some array libraries do not have the
`to_device` method.

# Parameters

x: array

: array instance from an array API compatible library.

device: device

: a `device` object (see the [Device Support](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/design_topics/device_support.html)
  section of the array API specification).

stream: Optional\[Union\[int, Any\]\]

: stream object to use during copy. In addition to the types supported
  in `array.__dlpack__`, implementations may choose to support any
  library-specific stream object with the caveat that any code using
  such an object would not be portable.

# Returns

out: array

: an array with the same data and data type as `x` and located on the
  specified `device`.

# Notes

For NumPy, this function effectively does nothing since the only supported
device is the CPU. For CuPy, this method supports CuPy CUDA
{external+cupy:class}`Device <cupy.cuda.Device>` and
{external+cupy:class}`Stream <cupy.cuda.Stream>` objects. For PyTorch,
this is the same as {external+torch:meth}`x.to(device) <torch.Tensor.to>`
(the `stream` argument is not supported in PyTorch).

# See Also

device : Hardware device the array data resides on.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers.size
documentation:
Return the total number of elements of x.

This is equivalent to `x.size` according to the [standard](https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.array.size.html).
This helper is included because PyTorch defines `size` in an
{external+torch:meth}`incompatible way <torch.Tensor.size>`.

classes.name: array_api_compat.common._helpers._dask_device
documentation: Placeholder object to represent the dask device
when the array backend is not the CPU.
(since it is not easy to tell which device a dask array is on)

functions.name: array_api_compat.cupy._aliases.asarray
documentation:
Array API compatibility wrapper for asarray().

See the corresponding documentation in the array library and/or the array API
specification for more details.
asarray also adds the copy keyword, which is not present in numpy 1.0.

functions.name: array_api_compat.numpy.linalg.solve
documentation: Note: unlike np.linalg.solve, the array API solve() only accepts x2 as a
vector when it is exactly 1-dimensional. All other cases treat x2 as a stack
of matrices. The np.linalg.solve behavior of allowing stacks of both
matrices and vectors is ambiguous c.f.
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/15349 and
https://github.com/data-apis/array-api/issues/285.
To workaround this, the below is the code from np.linalg.solve except
only calling solve1 in the exactly 1D case.
This code is here instead of in common because it is numpy specific. Also
note that CuPy's solve() does not currently support broadcasting (see
https://github.com/cupy/cupy/blob/main/cupy/cublas.py#L43).

modules.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases
documentation: These are functions that are just aliases of existing functions in NumPy.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.arange
documentation: These functions are modified from the NumPy versions.
Creation functions add the device keyword (which does nothing for NumPy)

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.std
documentation: These functions have different keyword argument names

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.cumulative_sum
documentation: cumulative_sum is renamed from cumsum, and adds the include_initial keyword
argument

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.clip
documentation: The min and max argument names in clip are different and not optional in numpy, and type
promotion behavior is different.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.permute_dims
documentation: Unlike transpose(), the axes argument to permute_dims() is required.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.reshape
documentation: np.reshape calls the keyword argument 'newshape' instead of 'shape'

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.argsort
documentation: The descending keyword is new in sort and argsort, and 'kind' replaced with
'stable'

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.nonzero
documentation: nonzero should error for zero-dimensional arrays

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.ceil
documentation: ceil, floor, and trunc return integers for integer inputs

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.matmul
documentation: linear algebra functions

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.matrix_transpose
documentation: Unlike transpose, matrix_transpose only transposes the last two axes.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.isdtype
documentation:
Returns a boolean indicating whether a provided dtype is of a specified data type `kind`.

Note that outside of this function, this compat library does not yet fully
support complex numbers.

See
<https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.isdtype.html>
for more details
isdtype is a new function in the 2022.12 array API specification.

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.unstack
documentation: unstack is a new function in the 2023.12 array API standard

functions.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.sign
documentation: numpy 1.26 does not use the standard definition for sign on complex numbers

classes.name: array_api_compat.common._aliases.UniqueAllResult
documentation: np.unique() is split into four functions in the array API:
unique_all, unique_counts, unique_inverse, and unique_values (this is done
to remove polymorphic return types).
The functions here return namedtuples (np.unique() returns a normal
tuple).
Note that these named tuples aren't actually part of the standard namespace,
but I don't see any issue with exporting the names here regardless.

modules.name: array_api_compat.cupy._info
documentation:
Array API Inspection namespace

This is the namespace for inspection functions as defined by the array API
standard. See
https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/inspection.html for
more details.

classes.name: array_api_compat.cupy._info.__array_namespace_info__

methods.name: array_api_compat.cupy._info.__array_namespace_info__.capabilities
documentation:
Return a dictionary of array API library capabilities.

The resulting dictionary has the following keys:

- **"boolean indexing"**: boolean indicating whether an array library
  supports boolean indexing. Always `True` for CuPy.
- **"data-dependent shapes"**: boolean indicating whether an array
  library supports data-dependent output shapes. Always `True` for
  CuPy.

See
<https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.info.capabilities.html>
for more details.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Returns

capabilities

: A dictionary of array API library capabilities.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = xp.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.capabilities()
{'boolean indexing': True,
 'data-dependent shapes': True}
```

methods.name: array_api_compat.cupy._info.__array_namespace_info__.default_device

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,

: The default device used for new CuPy arrays.


methods.name: array_api_compat.cupy._info.__array_namespace_info__.default_dtypes
documentation:
The default data types used for new CuPy arrays.

For CuPy, this always returns the following dictionary:

- **"real floating"**: `cupy.float64`
- **"complex floating"**: `cupy.complex128`
- **"integral"**: `cupy.intp`
- **"indexing"**: `cupy.intp`

# Parameters

device

: The device to get the default data types for.

# Returns

dtypes

: A dictionary describing the default data types used for new CuPy
  arrays.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = xp.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.default_dtypes()
{'real floating': cupy.float64,
 'complex floating': cupy.complex128,
 'integral': cupy.int64,
 'indexing': cupy.int64}
```

methods.name: array_api_compat.cupy._info.__array_namespace_info__.dtypes
documentation:
The array API data types supported by CuPy.

Note that this function only returns data types that are defined by
the array API.

# Parameters

device

: The device to get the data types for.

kind

: The kind of data types to return. If `None`, all data types are
  returned. If a string, only data types of that kind are returned.
  If a tuple, a dictionary containing the union of the given kinds
  is returned. The following kinds are supported:

  - `'bool'`: boolean data types (i.e., `bool`).
  - `'signed integer'`: signed integer data types (i.e., `int8`,
    `int16`, `int32`, `int64`).
  - `'unsigned integer'`: unsigned integer data types (i.e.,
    `uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `uint64`).
  - `'integral'`: integer data types. Shorthand for `('signed
    integer', 'unsigned integer')`.
  - `'real floating'`: real-valued floating-point data types
    (i.e., `float32`, `float64`).
  - `'complex floating'`: complex floating-point data types (i.e.,
    `complex64`, `complex128`).
  - `'numeric'`: numeric data types. Shorthand for `('integral',
    'real floating', 'complex floating')`.

# Returns

dtypes

: A dictionary mapping the names of data types to the corresponding
  CuPy data types.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = xp.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.dtypes(kind='signed integer')
{'int8': cupy.int8,
 'int16': cupy.int16,
 'int32': cupy.int32,
 'int64': cupy.int64}
```

methods.name: array_api_compat.cupy._info.__array_namespace_info__.devices
documentation:
The devices supported by CuPy.

Returns
-------
devices : list of str
    The devices supported by CuPy.

See Also
--------
__array_namespace_info__.capabilities,
__array_namespace_info__.default_device,
__array_namespace_info__.default_dtypes,
__array_namespace_info__.dtypes

Get the array API inspection namespace for CuPy.

The array API inspection namespace defines the following functions:

- capabilities()
- default_device()
- default_dtypes()
- dtypes()
- devices()

See
<https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/inspection.html>
for more details.

# Returns

info

: The array API inspection namespace for CuPy.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.default_dtypes()
{'real floating': cupy.float64,
 'complex floating': cupy.complex128,
 'integral': cupy.int64,
 'indexing': cupy.int64}
```

functions.name: array_api_compat.numpy._aliases.asarray
documentation:
Array API compatibility wrapper for asarray().

See the corresponding documentation in the array library and/or the array API
specification for more details.
asarray also adds the copy keyword, which is not present in numpy 1.0.
asarray() is different enough between numpy, cupy, and dask, the logic
complicated enough that it's easier to define it separately for each module
rather than trying to combine everything into one function in common/

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch.linalg.cross
documentation: Note: torch.linalg.cross does not default to axis=-1 (it defaults to the
first axis with size 3), see https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/58743
torch.cross also does not support broadcasting when it would add new
dimensions https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/39656

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch.linalg.trace
documentation: torch.trace doesn't support the offset argument and doesn't support stacking

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch.fft.fftn
documentation: Several torch fft functions do not map axes to dim

methods.name: array_api_compat.numpy._info.__array_namespace_info__.dtypes
documentation:
The array API data types supported by NumPy.

Note that this function only returns data types that are defined by
the array API.

# Parameters

device

: The device to get the data types for. For NumPy, only `'cpu'` is
  allowed.

kind

: The kind of data types to return. If `None`, all data types are
  returned. If a string, only data types of that kind are returned.
  If a tuple, a dictionary containing the union of the given kinds
  is returned. The following kinds are supported:

  - `'bool'`: boolean data types (i.e., `bool`).
  - `'signed integer'`: signed integer data types (i.e., `int8`,
    `int16`, `int32`, `int64`).
  - `'unsigned integer'`: unsigned integer data types (i.e.,
    `uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `uint64`).
  - `'integral'`: integer data types. Shorthand for `('signed
    integer', 'unsigned integer')`.
  - `'real floating'`: real-valued floating-point data types
    (i.e., `float32`, `float64`).
  - `'complex floating'`: complex floating-point data types (i.e.,
    `complex64`, `complex128`).
  - `'numeric'`: numeric data types. Shorthand for `('integral',
    'real floating', 'complex floating')`.

# Returns

dtypes

: A dictionary mapping the names of data types to the corresponding
  NumPy data types.

# See Also

\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.capabilities,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_device,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.default_dtypes,
\_\_array_namespace_info\_\_.devices

# Examples

```pycon
>>> info = np.__array_namespace_info__()
>>> info.dtypes(kind='signed integer')
{'int8': numpy.int8,
 'int16': numpy.int16,
 'int32': numpy.int32,
 'int64': numpy.int64}
```

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.max
documentation: These wrappers are mostly based on the fact that pytorch uses 'dim' instead
of 'axis'.
torch.min and torch.max return a tuple and don't support multiple axes https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/58745

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.sort
documentation: torch.sort also returns a tuple
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70921

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.concat
documentation: torch.concat doesn't support dim=None
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70925

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.squeeze
documentation: torch.squeeze only accepts int dim and doesn't require it
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70924. Support for tuple dim was
added at https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/89017.

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.broadcast_to
documentation: torch.broadcast_to uses size instead of shape

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.permute_dims
documentation: torch.permute uses dims instead of axes

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.flip
documentation: The axis parameter doesn't work for flip() and roll()
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/71210. Also torch.flip() doesn't
accept axis=None

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.reshape
documentation: torch.reshape doesn't have the copy keyword

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.arange
documentation: torch.arange doesn't support returning empty arrays
(https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70915), and doesn't support some
keyword argument combinations
(https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70914)

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.eye
documentation: torch.eye does not accept None as a default for the second argument and
doesn't support off-diagonals (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70910)

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.linspace
documentation: torch.linspace doesn't have the endpoint parameter

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.full
documentation: torch.full does not accept an int size
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70906

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.ones
documentation: ones, zeros, and empty do not accept shape as a keyword argument

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.tril
documentation: tril and triu do not call the keyword argument k

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.expand_dims
documentation: Functions that aren't in torch https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/58742

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.unique_all
documentation: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/70920

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.tensordot
documentation: torch.tensordot uses dims instead of axes

functions.name: array_api_compat.torch._aliases.isdtype
documentation:
Returns a boolean indicating whether a provided dtype is of a specified data type `kind`.

Note that outside of this function, this compat library does not yet fully
support complex numbers.

See
<https://data-apis.org/array-api/latest/API_specification/generated/array_api.isdtype.html>
for more details
